did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Synergic Deep Learning (SDL) model, which consists of dual pre-trained Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNNs) based on ResNet-50 and a synergic network, to perform skin lesion classification (benign vs. malignant) on dermoscopy images from the ISIC 2016 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel Synergic Deep Learning (SDL) model for skin lesion classification. It details the methodology (dual DCNNs, synergic network), describes the dataset used (ISIC 2016), and reports new empirical results (accuracy, average precision, AUC) comparing the proposed model against baselines and state-of-the-art methods. It is published in the MICCAI 2018 conference proceedings.

title: Skin Lesion Classification in Dermoscopy Images Using Synergic Deep Learning
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of skin lesion classification in dermoscopy images by proposing a Synergic Deep Learning (SDL) model to mitigate intra-class variation and inter-class similarity. The SDL architecture utilizes dual pre-trained ResNet-50 deep convolutional neural networks that mutually learn from each other via a synergic network predicting class similarity. The model was trained and evaluated on the ISIC 2016 Skin Lesion Classification dataset, which contains 900 training and 379 test images labeled as benign or malignant. Evaluation metrics included accuracy, average precision, and area under the ROC curve, comparing the SDL model against baseline ResNet-50 and ensemble methods. The proposed SDL model achieved an accuracy of 85.75% and an average precision of 0.664, outperforming existing state-of-the-art methods on the dataset.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two binary classes in the ISIC 2016 dataset used for classification. The training set contained 727 benign lesion images and the test set contained 304 benign images.

One of the two binary classes in the ISIC 2016 dataset used for classification. The training set contained 173 malignant lesion images and the test set contained 75 malignant images. The paper also references 'melanoma' in the context of reducing deaths and diagnosing malignant carcinomas/melanomas in related work.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnosis of skin lesions as benign or malignant
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification of dermoscopy images into 'benign' or 'malignant' categories using the ISIC 2016 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels (benign vs. malignant) and outputs class probabilities via a softmax layer, with the final class label determined by argmax of these probabilities. Evaluation metrics include accuracy, average precision, and AUC.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2016 Skin Lesion Classification dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset released by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC), consisting of dermoscopic images screened for privacy and quality assurance, with gold standard malignancy diagnoses.

use.training: Used for training the Synergic Deep Learning (SDL) model, including data augmentation (random rotation and horizontal/vertical flips) to enlarge the dataset.
use.validation: Used for hyperparameter tuning (specifically for the trade-off parameter lambda) as indicated by performance curves on the validation set.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the SDL model and comparison against baseline methods (ResNet-50, ResNet-502) and state-of-the-art challenge records.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To visualize and compare the performance of the proposed SDL model against the ensemble of two ResNet-50 models on the test set.
To quantify and compare the classification performance of the SDL model, ResNet-50, and ResNet-502 on the test set.
To evaluate and compare the performance of the SDL model against state-of-the-art methods and challenge records, serving as the primary ranking metric for the challenge.
To evaluate the performance of the SDL model, ResNet-50, and ResNet-502 on the test set.
To determine the optimal value for the trade-off parameter lambda by monitoring the Average Precision on the validation set as lambda varied.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using accuracy, average precision, and AUC metrics without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2016 Skin Lesion Classification dat.evaluation_setting: Comparison against Ensemble Learning (ResNet-502) and State-of-the-Art Challenge Records
ISIC 2016 Skin Lesion Classification dat.notes: The SDL model with data augmentation achieved the highest AP (0.6644) and Acc (0.8575) among the compared models in the internal comparison. In the external comparison against top five challenge records, the SDL model achieved the highest AP (0.664) and Acc (0.858), and the second highest AUC (0.818). The paper notes that SDL outperforms ResNet-502 regarding three evaluation metrics regardless of data augmentation. Hyperparameter lambda was set to 3 based on validation set performance.

architecture_details.components: Dual DCNN components (DCNN-A and DCNN-B) and a synergic network
architecture_details.dual_dcnn_modifications: Original classification layer replaced with a fully connected layer of 1024 neurons, a fully connected layer of K (number of classes) neurons, and a softmax layer. Parameters initialized by sampling a uniform distribution U(-0.05, 0.05).
architecture_details.synergic_network_structure: Embedding layer, fully connected learning layer, and output layer. Takes concatenated deep features from the penultimate fully connected layer of DCNN-A and DCNN-B as input.
architecture_details.parameter_sharing: Parameters not shared between DCNN-A and DCNN-B during optimization.

loss_functions: Cross-entropy loss for classification (l(A) and l(B))
Binary cross-entropy loss for synergic network (l(S))

hyperparameters.learning_rate_schedule: Variable learning rate: eta(t) = eta(0) / (1 + 10^-4 * t)

classification_or_output_head.final_prediction: Argmax of the sum of probabilistic predictions from both DCNN components

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 224 × 224 × 3 using bicubic interpolation
Data augmentation including random rotation and horizontal/vertical flips

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to training images to enlarge the dataset and alleviate overfitting. Specific rotation angles or probabilities were not reported.
Applied to training images to enlarge the dataset and alleviate overfitting. Specific probabilities were not reported.
Applied to training images to enlarge the dataset and alleviate overfitting. Specific probabilities were not reported.

conclusions: The proposed Synergic Deep Learning (SDL) model, which uses dual pre-trained DCNNs and a synergic network to enable mutual learning, addresses the challenges of intra-class variation and inter-class similarity in skin lesion classification.
The SDL model achieved state-of-the-art performance on the ISIC 2016 Skin Lesion Classification dataset, with an accuracy of 85.75% and an average precision of 0.664.

The synergic learning strategy contributes significantly to higher performance compared to ensemble learning methods (ResNet-502) without synergic learning, as demonstrated by steady improvements across accuracy, average precision, and AUC metrics.

The SDL model outperformed the top five challenge records in the ISIC 2016 leaderboard, achieving the highest average precision and accuracy, and the second highest AUC, without using segmentation networks.
The hyperparameter lambda, which balances classification error and synergic error, was empirically determined to be 3 for optimal performance.

While extending the model to SDLn (n > 2) provides performance gains, the computational time cost grows significantly; therefore, using SDL2 or SDL3 is suggested to balance performance and complexity.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a single dataset (ISIC 2016) with a small test set (379 images), raising concerns about external validity and generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance in the training set (727 benign vs. 173 malignant), which may bias the model towards the majority class despite the use of Average Precision as a metric.
No formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the superiority of the proposed SDL model over baselines or challenge records.
The model relies on pre-trained weights from ImageNet, which may introduce domain bias as dermoscopy images differ significantly from natural images.

Data leakage risk exists because the synergic network is trained on pairs of images from the training set; if the pairing strategy or hyperparameter tuning (lambda=3) was not strictly validated on a held-out set, performance estimates may be optimistic.
The paper does not report computational complexity metrics (e.g., FLOPs, inference time) for the proposed SDL model, only relative training time costs for extended SDLn models.

The model was not compared against segmentation-based methods that were used by top challenge participants, potentially underestimating the performance gap if segmentation is critical for clinical utility.

The hyperparameter lambda was selected based on validation set performance, but the specific split of the 900 training images into training and validation sets is not explicitly detailed, risking data leakage if the split was not rigorous.
